text:
2.

“I thought you weren’t coming,” Will said, shoving a pint toward Caleb as he sagged onto the stool beside him.

William Nealy had his father’s fire-red hair and brown eyes, but his features were finer, his pale skin dusted with freckles. He was twenty-six—Caleb’s age. They’d met at the very start of university and had been inseparable ever since, even through Will’s many changes of major. His burgundy coat hung over the back of the stool with his satchel, which always held a notebook in case inspiration ambushed him on the way.

“Sorry, it ran late,” Caleb said.

“I know, I know. You work too much. That old man is wringing you dry,” Will said with a grin, taking a long pull of beer.

The Bronze Kraken—named for the huge bronze tentacles that climbed the back bar and curled across the ceiling—was crowded most nights with students and alumni like them. People who preferred this clamorous refuge to genteel salons and the long shadows of their fathers and elder brothers. Not that either of them had titles. The Nealys had grown rich in trade but never received a patent of nobility; in the last hundred years, since the northern islands formed the Union and declared the republic, there hadn’t been anyone to grant one. Caleb’s father had been disowned by his own kin when he came home with an exotic woman from the Colonies and, brazen as you please, married her. Friends’ goodwill got him back on his feet, and later he repaid every favor twice over. Popularity, however, brought neither rank nor family forgiveness. Not that Will or Caleb much cared.

Will fished a few paper-wrapped cigarettes from his coat and lit one. Caleb didn’t usually smoke, but on nights like this he accepted one or two.

“Now this is living. Tell me what’s new with you.”

“Last time you said I wasn’t allowed to talk about work when we drink,” Caleb said.

“Don’t tell me it’s only work with you. What about Miss Fawns? Have you asked the question yet? I know the arrangements are practically done and her father has settled matters with yours, but there has to be a drop of romance in you.”

Caleb sighed. Heloise Fawns—and the engagement arranged around her—was a topic he preferred to postpone.

“I haven’t had time to buy a ring. Too much going on, and I don’t know… I’m not even sure she likes me, even a little. I want it to be perfect. Maybe then she’ll smile once.”

He did want to get Heloise something truly beautiful, only he had no idea what kind of ring she would want. When they met, she was always kind to him, but the kindness was polite and overly formal. Caleb prided himself on reading people: he caught moods easily and saw through lies fast. Heloise remained a closed book. This wasn’t the cool, distant marriage he imagined for himself, not when his parents had loved each other with such heat. On the other hand, the girl was respectable, from a good family, and met every expectation of a promising wife. Not least, her father was willing to give her to Caleb despite the man’s foreign cast. His skin was a few shades darker than most here; his hair and stubble were probably black or very dark brown—he hadn’t seen either in so long he couldn’t be sure—and his eyes were large, cat-curved, emerald green like his mother’s.

“I don’t get it,” William said, shaking his head. “I remember women liked you, until they got tired of your endless lectures about the sea.”

“Sure. Just not the women actually looking for a husband,” Caleb laughed. “It’s better this way. Miss Fawns won’t mind if I spend more time with Lord Ebbing than with her, my father will be satisfied, and Mr. Fawns has always been cordial. So I suppose he will be too.”

Will studied his beer, where a gas lamp’s warm light shivered back at him. In the corner, a fiddler and a singer slipped into a slow, melancholy tune, as if the room’s mood had tuned their strings.

“And is that enough for you?” he asked at last, almost softly.

Caleb blinked. He hadn’t expected a question that careful from his loud, fun-seeking friend.

“What do you mean?”

“It’s just—every time you talk about what would make other people happy. You never say what you want. So is it really enough that everyone else is pleased with you? Hold on, I’ll order something short. Think while I do.”

Caleb tried. No matter how he pushed, all that rose was the roar of waves, the smell of salt, the cold bite of water on his hands. The distance over the swell. The horizon he’d decided would never be his.

The shots came. They clinked glasses and tossed them back. The whisky burned going down, then pooled warm in his stomach.

“I don’t have time for deep thoughts,” he said finally. “For now I’m content to do my job well and not make enemies. This past month has been a nightmare.”

“All right, you’re clearly at the end of your rope. Break the rule—tell me what’s weighing on you,” Will sighed, theatrically. “But make it so I can follow. No jargon.”

“If only jargon were the trouble,” Caleb said, rubbing his brow and his eyes. He really was spent. “I thought the hearing would settle it, but the shipping companies keep hounding Ebbing to give them something. He’s trying, but… he can’t. Nothing better than ordering the engineers about for now. We keep going back to the manuals, sit up half the night spinning theories, and we’re groping in the dark. I’m saying this as your friend, not officially.”

“I know. Everyone’s tense. My father says three of his ships are stuck in port—he doesn’t dare send them out. Especially since they found a wreck yesterday, ten miles south of here, and they say it belongs to someone from the Western Shipping Company. Not my father’s, of course, so—”

“Wait, slow down!” Caleb’s eyes lit. “You said there’s a wreck?”

“There is…?”

“Have they hauled it away yet?”

“Hardly. I told you they found it only yesterday, and it’s probably beyond saving anyway.”

Caleb’s thoughts picked up speed. A wreck. It might hold traces, something to tell them what they were up against.

“I have to tell Ebbing at once,” he said, pushing up from the stool, but William caught his arm.

“No. You’ve worked enough today. I finally dragged you here—do you think I’m letting you go? That wreck will wait. This beer will go warm.”

“What if they take it away in the meantime…”

Will heaved a theatrical sigh. “If I get my father to send a carriage for you tomorrow and take you there, will you sit, drink this miserable pint, and not say one word about work for the rest of the evening?”

Caleb grinned and sat back down. “You might be saving lives right now, Will.”

“Sure, sure. Mostly yours.” He clinked his pint to Caleb’s, and both of them drank, then ordered another round.
